attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a four-hour history of progressively worsening shortness of breath and wheezing. She has a history of asthma since childhood. She uses a salbutamol inhaler as needed but has been using it more frequently over the past week due to a viral upper respiratory tract infection. She has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears distressed and is using accessory muscles to breathe. Her pulse rate is 125/min, blood pressure 140/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 38/min, SpO2 91% on 15 L/min oxygen via non-rebreather mask, and temperature 37.2°C. Chest auscultation reveals diffuse expiratory wheezes with poor air entry bilaterally. She has received three doses of nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium bromide over the past hour with minimal improvement. She has also received intravenous hydrocortisone.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with severe acute asthma, or 'status asthmaticus', a life-threatening asthma exacerbation that is not responding to initial bronchodilator therapy and systemic corticosteroids. The next step in management should focus on additional pharmacological interventions to improve bronchodilation and reduce airway inflammation before considering more invasive measures.

Intravenous magnesium sulfate is the most appropriate next step in this scenario. It acts as a smooth muscle relaxant and can provide additional bronchodilation in patients with severe asthma exacerbations who have not responded adequately to inhaled beta-agonists, anticholinergics, and systemic corticosteroids. A dose of 10-20 mmol IV magnesium sulfate is recommended in this setting.

Intravenous adrenaline infusion is a consideration in severe asthma, particularly in cases with an allergic trigger, or when other treatments have failed. However, it is typically reserved for when other measures have failed, or the patient has other symptoms such as hypotension or shock.

Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) modalities such as CPAP and BiPAP have a well-established role in the treatment of exacerbations of COPD, but their role in severe acute asthma is controversial and not well-established. Whilst it may be considered as an oxygenation strategy to avoid intubation, it would not be the next step at this point.

Intubation and mechanical ventilation is a last resort in status asthmaticus due to the high risk of complications. It should only be considered if there are signs of impending respiratory failure or altered mental status, which are not present in this case.

Intravenous aminophylline infusion is no longer recommended as a first-line treatment in acute severe asthma due to its narrow therapeutic index and potential for significant side effects. It may be considered in specific cases where other treatments have failed, but it would not be the next step in this scenario.

In addition to magnesium sulfate, management should include continuous nebulised salbutamol, systemic corticosteroids (which have already been administered), and close monitoring for signs of deterioration. If the patient fails to improve, escalation to intravenous bronchodilators or consideration of intubation may be necessary.
learningPoints: Severe acute asthma, or status asthmaticus, is a life-threatening emergency necessitating immediate and aggressive treatment.
Intravenous magnesium sulfate is an important adjunct therapy in severe asthma exacerbations not responding to initial bronchodilator treatment and systemic corticosteroids.
A stepwise approach to severe asthma management includes oxygen, inhaled bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids, and then consideration of adjunct therapies before invasive ventilation.
references: National Asthma Council Australia. (2025). Managing acute asthma in adults and adolescents in the ED. Australian Asthma Handbook: The National Guidelines for Health Professionals (Version 3.0). Retrieved 23 September 2025, from https://www.asthmahandbook.org.au/acute-asthma/adults-and-adolescents/managing-acute-asthma-in-adults-and-adolescents-in-the-ed

Kostakou E, Kaniaris E, Filiou E, Vasileiadis I, Katsaounou P, Tzortzaki E, Koulouris N, Koutsoukou A, Rovina N. Acute Severe Asthma in Adolescent and Adult Patients: Current Perspectives on Assessment and Management. J Clin Med. 2019 Aug 22;8(9):1283. doi: 10.3390/jcm8091283. PMID: 31443563; PMCID: PMC6780340. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31443563/

Chakraborty RK, Chen RJ, Basnet S. Status Asthmaticus. [Updated 2024 Feb 9]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526070/
